title: Should There Be a Legal Minimum Age of 16 for Personal Smartphone Ownership?
prompt: Evaluate whether jurisdictions should legally restrict personal smartphone ownership — not just social media accounts — to age 16 or older. Consider Jonathan Haidt's adolescent mental health arguments, the causal vs correlational debate on phones and teen depression, enforcement feasibility, equity concerns for kids in unsafe neighborhoods or custody situations, the distinction between smartphones and dumbphones, parental rights, and whether a legal floor would meaningfully change behavior given coordination problems among parents.

agents.bio: Clinician who has practiced adolescent psychiatry since 2005 and watched her caseload of middle-school girls with self-harm and suicidal ideation quintuple after 2012. Finds the Haidt thesis clinically obvious even where the epidemiology is contested. Supports a hard legal floor because she has watched parents try and fail to hold the line alone.

Media studies scholar who wrote a book on technology moral panics from the penny dreadful to video games to TikTok. Argues the 'phones cause depression' literature suffers from severe specification-searching and that the Haidt consensus evaporates once you control for survey mode changes and pandemic effects. Deeply skeptical of banning a general-purpose tool.

Labor economist who studies adolescent time use and has published causal IV estimates of social media's effects on teen outcomes. Finds modest negative effects for girls, null for boys, and thinks a 16-year floor is a blunt instrument when account-level restrictions would target the actual harm more cleanly.

Policy director at a low-income family advocacy group. Argues a smartphone ban punishes kids in single-parent households, unsafe neighborhoods, and custody transitions who rely on phones for logistics and safety. Points out that affluent parents can substitute watches, tutors, and chauffeurs — poor families cannot.

Former school principal turned education policy consultant who watched the Wait Until 8th parent pledge collapse in every affluent district she advised. Believes the only way out of the parental prisoner's dilemma is a legal norm — not because phones are uniquely evil but because voluntary coordination at the parent level is structurally impossible at scale.
